players.name: Ørjan Nyland
players.bio: Nyland's 71 caps tell the story of a reliable, experienced shot-stopper who's guarded Norway's net for over a decade. The Volda-born veteran joined Sevilla after spells in England and brings World Cup experience that money can't buy.
players.facts: Born the same year Germany reunified. At 35, he's Norway's elder statesman between the sticks at this World Cup.

players.name: Morten Thorsby
players.bio: A combative central midfielder with a conscience — Thorsby is as well known for his environmental activism as his pressing game. The Oslo native plays his club football in Italy and brings grit to Norway's engine room.
players.facts: Thorsby once skipped a flight to reduce his carbon footprint, taking a train instead. Climate hero, football warrior.

players.name: Kristoffer Ajer
players.bio: The towering Brentford defender uses his imposing 6'4" frame to dominate aerially while remaining comfortable on the ball — a true modern centre-back. Still only 28, Ajer has been a regular for Norway since his teens.
players.facts: Ajer was a promising basketball player in his youth. Thankfully for Norway, he chose football.

players.name: Leo Østigård
players.bio: Østigård is a combative, no-nonsense defender who makes life miserable for strikers. The Åndalsnes-born centre-back has earned his place through grit and consistency, representing Norway at this level before his 27th birthday.
players.facts: His surname means 'eastern farm' in Norwegian. Nothing farmyard about his defending, mind.

players.name: David Møller Wolfe
players.bio: The Bergen-born left-back is one of Norway's brightest young defenders, combining sharp attacking instincts with solid defensive work at Wolves. At just 24, this is likely the first of many World Cup appearances for Møller Wolfe.
players.facts: His double-barrelled surname sounds like a folk band. His overlapping runs are more rock 'n' roll than folk.

players.name: Patrick Berg
players.bio: Bodø/Glimt's homegrown anchor, Berg is the tireless defensive midfielder who lets Norway's stars shine by doing the unglamorous work brilliantly. A loyal servant to his hometown club and a crucial cog in the national setup.
players.facts: Berg plays club football above the Arctic Circle at Bodø/Glimt — one of European football's most northerly top-flight clubs.

players.name: Alexander Sørloth
players.bio: The towering Atlético Madrid striker is Norway's second-highest scorer and a physical handful for any defence. Sørloth has reinvented himself at elite level after a rocky start to his career, proving doubters spectacularly wrong.
players.facts: Sørloth scored 33 goals on loan at Trabzonspor in a single season — a record that made Europe sit up and take notice.

players.name: Sander Berge
players.bio: Born on Valentine's Day, Berge's love for the game is evident in every powerful stride. The rangy Fulham midfielder is equally effective breaking up play and driving forward, making him one of Norway's most complete midfielders.
players.facts: Berge was Sheffield United's record signing when he joined for £22m in 2020. Romance was very much in the air.

players.name: Erling Haaland
players.bio: The most feared striker on the planet — born in Leeds but raised Norwegian, Haaland has shattered records at every club. His 55 international goals in 50 caps make him Norway's all-time top scorer by some distance.
players.kit: Nike Mercurial Vapor XIII Elite FG
players.facts: Haaland was born in Leeds while his father Alfie played for Leeds United. He repays that debt by terrorising English defenders.

players.name: Martin Ødegaard
players.bio: Arsenal's elegant captain is Norway's creative heartbeat — a technically gifted playmaker who reads the game like a chess grandmaster. Ødegaard was once the world's most expensive teenager; he's since become worth every penny.
players.facts: Ødegaard signed for Real Madrid aged 16. He's now Arsenal's captain. The teenage hype was, for once, completely justified.

players.name: Jørgen Strand Larsen
players.bio: The Crystal Palace striker is a powerful, intelligent forward who links play well and has a poacher's instinct. Strand Larsen has broken into the Norwegian setup emphatically and offers a strong alternative to Haaland — no pressure there.
players.facts: Being Norway's backup striker means playing second fiddle to Haaland. Still, 6 goals in 28 caps is nothing to sniff at.

players.name: Sander Tangvik
players.bio: The young Hamburger SV goalkeeper heads into this World Cup uncapped at senior level but has made waves through Norway's youth ranks. A promising shot-stopper with good reflexes, Tangvik is here to learn from the best.
players.facts: Tangvik is yet to earn a senior cap — making him one of the youngest and most uncapped goalkeepers at World Cup 2026.

players.name: Egil Selvik
players.bio: Selvik has made his name as a dependable goalkeeper in the English Championship with Watford. With 7 caps to his name, the third-choice keeper provides solid competition and cover for Norway's number one slot.
players.facts: Selvik kept goal for Haugesund before moving to England — a journey from the Norwegian coast to the Watford bench.

players.name: Fredrik Aursnes
players.bio: Aursnes is a versatile, high-energy midfielder who thrives in Benfica's pressing system. Equally effective in defence or attack, he's a manager's dream utility player — the kind who makes the team work without always grabbing headlines.
players.facts: Aursnes can play in almost any midfield position. Coaches love him. Opponents can't quite work out where he'll turn up next.

players.name: Fredrik André Bjørkan
players.bio: Bjørkan is an attacking left-back who loves to bomb forward and has been a mainstay of Bodø/Glimt's European adventures. The Bodø native brings local pride and genuine quality to Norway's defensive options.
players.facts: Bjørkan has played UEFA Conference and Europa League football with Bodø/Glimt — not bad for a lad from the Arctic Circle.

players.name: Marcus Holmgren Pedersen
players.bio: Born in Hammerfest — one of the northernmost towns in the world — Pedersen has journeyed south to Serie A with Torino. A dynamic right-back with energy to burn, he loves to overlap and contribute going forward.
players.facts: Pedersen hails from Hammerfest, one of the world's northernmost cities. Now he plays his football in sunny Turin. Quite the commute.

players.name: Torbjørn Heggem
players.bio: The Bologna centre-back is a composed, ball-playing defender who has developed under one of European football's most progressive coaches. Heggem is establishing himself as a genuine option in Norway's defensive unit at just 27.
players.facts: His father Vegard Heggem played for Liverpool in the late 1990s. Norway clearly runs in the family — and left-back genes too.

players.name: Kristian Thorstvedt
players.bio: Son of Norwegian football legend Erik Thorstvedt, Kristian has carved his own path with consistent performances in Serie A. A box-to-box midfielder with technical quality and a nose for goal, he's a key creative option for Norway.
players.facts: His father Erik Thorstvedt kept goal for Tottenham in the early 1990s. The Thorstvedts are Norway's most reliable footballing family.

players.name: Thelo Aasgaard
players.bio: Born in Liverpool but proudly Norwegian, Aasgaard burst onto the scene with a remarkable goals-to-caps ratio for his country. The Rangers attacking midfielder is direct, creative, and clearly has no fear of the big occasion.
players.facts: Aasgaard has 5 goals in just 8 caps — a ratio that even Haaland would admire. Born in Liverpool, scoring for Norway. Football is weird.

players.name: Antonio Nusa
players.bio: Antonio Nusa is arguably the most exciting young talent in Norwegian football — a pacey, direct winger who joined RB Leipzig as a teenager. At just 21, he already has 8 international goals and a World Cup to announce himself to the world.
players.facts: Nusa was only 17 when he made his senior Norway debut. He was born in 2005 — younger than some players' professional contracts.

players.name: Andreas Schjelderup
players.bio: The Benfica forward is one of the most exciting young strikers in European football. Schjelderup's intelligent movement and technical quality have drawn comparisons to players well above his years. This World Cup is his stage.
players.facts: Schjelderup was born in Bodø — the same city producing Glimt's Champions League dreams. Something in that Arctic water.

players.name: Oscar Bobb
players.bio: After developing at Manchester City's academy, Bobb has emerged as a tricky, pacy wide player with an eye for a decisive moment. Now at Fulham, the Oslo-born winger brings Premier League experience and youthful flair to Norway's attack.
players.facts: Bobb spent years in Manchester City's famous academy system. He learned from the best — now he's showing it on the world stage.

players.name: Jens Petter Hauge
players.bio: Hauge made headlines by earning a move to AC Milan from Bodø/Glimt after dazzling in the Europa League. The left winger has since returned to his hometown club, where he continues to illuminate Scandinavian nights with his skill.
players.facts: Hauge went from Bodø/Glimt to AC Milan in 2020 after one brilliant European campaign. The Arctic to the San Siro is quite a leap.

players.name: Sondre Langås
players.bio: The Derby County defender has only three caps but is part of Norway's next defensive generation. Langås brings Championship experience and composure on the ball — a solid squad option who could have a big future in red and white.
players.facts: Langås plays for Derby County — a club with a historic World Cup connection. Ram-bunctious defending is clearly his speciality.

players.name: Henrik Falchener
players.bio: Falchener is the most uncapped outfield player in this Norway squad with just one senior appearance, but his inclusion reflects the coaches' faith in his potential. The Viking defender is young, composed and hungry to prove himself.
players.facts: Falchener plays for a club literally called Viking. With one cap to his name, his World Cup saga is only just beginning.

players.name: Julian Ryerson
players.bio: The Borussia Dortmund right-back is a dynamic, hard-working defender who has thrived in the Bundesliga. Ryerson brings European pedigree and Champions League experience to Norway's squad — no small thing at a first World Cup.
players.facts: Ryerson won the DFB-Pokal with Dortmund. He's experienced big nights in yellow — now it's Norway red's turn.
